The larger mill buildings, now the Hotel Millwright (opened 2020), surround the working mill. Shop open daily; mill tours by arrangement on Maifest and Oktoberfest Saturdays.

practical_info.admission: free admission to shop; mill tours by arrangement

visits.name: Amana Church (Hauptamana)
visits.text: The Amana churches express Pietist humility in architecture: no steeple, no stained glass, plain brick walls, wooden benches. The church in Main Amana is the most accessible and best-preserved. Services are still held by the Amana Church Society. Visitors are welcome outside service hours. The contrast with mainstream American church design is immediate.

activities.text: You follow the 3.1-mile Kolonieweg (Colony Way) trail from the Amana depot along the Mill Race canal that once powered the colonies' mills. It connects Main Amana to Middle Amana and circles the 170-acre Lily Lake, where you have a good chance of spotting trumpeter swans and river otters. A second trail cuts through the Amana Forest along an Iowa River bluff. Both trails are flat and easy; free admission.

practical_info.tip: bring binoculars for Lily Lake - swans and otters are common in spring and fall

activities.text: You make your way through Ackerman Winery, operating since 1956 - Iowa's oldest working winery, now run by the Wyant family since 2015. Village Winery and White Cross Cellars both offer tastings within the same cluster of villages. For dinner, the Ronneburg Restaurant, named after the 13th-century German castle where the Inspirationists once took refuge, serves traditional German and American dishes. The whole circuit covers less than three miles of driving.

practical_info.tip: Maifest (May) and Oktoberfest (October) add live music and lederhosen - the busiest weekends of the year

map_keywords: National Czech Slovak Museum Cedar Rapids

visits.name: National Czech & Slovak Museum & Library
visits.text: You walk into a 50,000-square-foot Smithsonian Affiliate on the Cedar River's south bank, which began in 1978 as the Czech Fine Arts Foundation. Its showpiece is the largest collection of traditional Czech and Slovak kroje (folk costumes) outside the Czech Republic and Slovakia, including pieces dating to the 16th century. The Immigrant Home exhibit - a surviving 19th-century Bohemian house relocated to higher ground after the 2008 Cedar River flood - walks you through five generations of one family's life. Paid entry.

visits.name: Cedar Rapids Museum of Art (Grant Wood Collection)
visits.text: Cedar Rapids-born Grant Wood painted American Gothic in 1930 in a studio in downtown Cedar Rapids. The museum holds the world's largest public collection of Wood's work, from early Impressionist canvases to the regionalist paintings that made him famous. The Grant Wood Studio is a separate site a few blocks away - book studio access in advance. Paid entry.

practical_info.tip: Grant Wood Studio access requires a separate booking - check crmoa.org for current availability

visits.name: Old Capitol Museum, Iowa City
visits.text: You drive 30 miles south to Iowa's first state capitol, which served the legislative assembly from 1842 until 1857, when the capital moved to Des Moines. It then became the anchor of the University of Iowa campus - a National Historic Landmark on the Pentacrest. The cornerstone was laid in 1840, and the restored period rooms inside represent the 1840s-1850s appearance. Free admission; closed Sundays and Mondays.

activities.text: You push the door of Prairie Lights, founded in 1978 by Jim Harris and moved in 1982 to its current 11,000-square-foot space - the same building where Iowa City's literary society met in the 1930s, hosting Robert Frost, Carl Sandburg, and Langston Hughes. Seven Nobel Prize laureates in literature have read here, including Toni Morrison and J.M. Coetzee. The Live from Prairie Lights reading series runs four or five nights a week. Check the calendar before you go.

practical_info.tip: evening reading events are free most nights - check the schedule before you go

activities.text: You wander along 16th Avenue SW through Cedar Rapids' Czech Village, past the NCSML, where North America's only Orloj - a replica of Prague's medieval astronomical clock - stands in front of the museum. The adjacent NewBo (New Bohemia) district has converted industrial buildings into a food market, breweries, and galleries. You can walk both neighborhoods in under an hour.

practical_info.tip: NewBo City Market is open Saturdays year-round and Thursdays in summer

map_keywords: Monument aux tertres de Effigy Mounds

visits.name: Effigy Mounds National Monument
visits.text: You drive 45 miles north to this 2,526-acre monument, established in 1949, which preserves 206 earthen mounds along the Mississippi River bluffs in northeastern Iowa. Of the 206, 31 are effigy mounds built between 500 BCE and 1300 CE in the shapes of bears and birds - a cultural practice found only in the Upper Mississippi Valley. Great Bear Mound measures 137 feet long and 70 feet wide. The Marching Bear Group - ten bear and three bird effigies - stretches nearly a quarter mile along a bluff top. Fourteen miles of trails; free admission.

practical_info.distance: 45 miles north of Dubuque on Highway 76
practical_info.tip: fall foliage (mid-october) makes the bluff trails exceptional; go early on weekdays

visits.name: National Mississippi River Museum & Aquarium
visits.text: Iowa's most-visited attraction is a Smithsonian Affiliate covering the history and ecology of the Mississippi across multiple buildings at the Port of Dubuque. Six aquariums hold river creatures from giant catfish to river otters. Indoor galleries trace the steamboat era, locks-and-dams construction, and riverman culture. The preserved Fred W. Woodward riverboat is docked outside. Paid entry.

practical_info.tip: the Shot Tower and Fenelon Place Elevator are within 10 minutes on foot

visits.name: Dubuque Shot Tower
visits.text: You walk along the riverfront to the only surviving shot tower west of the Mississippi River - a 120-foot masonry tower built in 1856, on the National Register of Historic Places. The tower produced lead shot for muskets for only two years before being outmaneuvered by a St. Louis competitor. Restored in 2006 after standing abandoned since 1911. A historical marker tells the full story.

practical_info.location: Bell Street near the Mississippi Riverwalk

visits.name: Fenelon Place Elevator
visits.text: You board what is billed as the world's shortest, steepest scenic railway - built in July 1882 by former Dubuque mayor J.K. Graves, who was tired of the long buggy route up the bluff. A fire destroyed the original in 1884; Graves rebuilt and opened it to the public. The 296-foot bluff ride takes a minute each way, and the view at the top takes in Dubuque, the Mississippi, and parts of Wisconsin and Illinois simultaneously. Fee applies.

activities.text: You follow the Great River Road National Scenic Byway along the Iowa bank of the Mississippi from Dubuque south through Bellevue and Sabula. The 40-mile stretch between Dubuque and Guttenberg is particularly dramatic: sandstone bluffs drop to the river, lock-and-dam structures are visible from roadside pullouts, and small river towns have preserved 19th-century commercial streets. Free admission; drive at your own pace.

practical_info.tip: Guttenberg, IA, 40 miles south, has an intact limestone commercial district from the 1840s and river views from its levee park

activities.text: The Port of Dubuque's revitalized riverwalk and entertainment district surrounds the Mississippi River Museum at the foot of the bluffs. Stone Cliff Winery, in the historic Dubuque Star Brewing Company building, is one of the most atmospheric dinner options. The view of the river and old warehouse brick architecture after dark is a fitting close to a day on the Mississippi.

practical_info.tip: Stone Cliff Winery is in the historic Star Brewing building, a short walk from the Museum

visits.name: Ulysses S. Grant Home State Historic Site
visits.text: You walk up Bouthillier Street to an Italianate brick house built in 1859-60 and presented to Grant by local Republicans on his return from the Civil War, August 18, 1865. Grant used it as his official voting address during the 1868 presidential campaign and visited through his presidency (1869-1877), returning for the last time in 1880. His children gave the house to Galena in 1904 as a memorial. Original furnishings remain throughout. Interpreter-led tours run Wednesday through Sunday.

visits.name: Galena Main Street Historic District
visits.text: You wander along Main Street past the DeSoto House Hotel, opened in 1855, where both Abraham Lincoln and Stephen Douglas stayed and spoke during the 1858 campaign. More than 125 shops and restaurants occupy intact 19th-century commercial buildings along the steep half-mile. The Galena & U.S. Grant Museum holds Grant memorabilia including Thomas Nast's 1895 painting Peace in Union - 9 feet tall and 12 feet wide. Museum paid entry; the street itself is free.

practical_info.admission: paid entry for museum; free to walk Main Street
practical_info.tip: Galena Trolley Tours offers a 45-minute narrated circuit - a good orientation before exploring on foot

activities.text: Galena Cellars Vineyard sits on rolling farmland in the Galena driftless country, a few miles south of downtown. The Driftless Region - untouched by the last glacial advance - gives this corner of Illinois and Iowa its unusual topography: limestone ridges and creek-cut hollows unlike the flat plains to the south. The vineyard produces cold-hardy varietals; an outdoor patio offers long views over the hills. A downtown tasting room on Main Street is also open.

practical_info.tip: vineyard is about 6 miles south of downtown - call ahead to confirm seasonal hours

activities.text: You drive 20 miles east of Galena to Apple River Canyon, where the river cuts 200 feet deep through Silurian dolomite limestone - geology dating roughly 430 million years ago, with fossils exposed along the creek. Five trails cover the canyon rim and valley floor. It's one of the most dramatically non-flat landscapes in the region. Fee applies.

practical_info.tip: the east canyon trail (~1.5 miles) reaches the best rim views with moderate effort

visits.name: Anderson Japanese Gardens
visits.text: You wander through 12 acres of koi ponds, cascading waterfalls, winding gravel paths, and trained Japanese maples along Spring Creek. Every rock, tree placement, and water feature was designed by master craftsman Hoichi Kurisu, who has been involved since 1978. The gardens are dense enough for 90 minutes without retracing steps. The on-site cafe Fresco at the Gardens serves locally sourced Asian-inspired lunch. Paid entry.

practical_info.tip: arrive before noon to avoid weekend afternoon crowds

activities.text: Downtown Rockford's Burpee Museum is home to Jane, one of the most complete juvenile Tyrannosaurus rex fossils ever found, unearthed in South Dakota in 2001. The permanent dinosaur gallery built around Jane has won national attention for its interpretive approach. The museum is a 10-minute drive from Anderson Gardens. Paid entry.

practical_info.tip: combine with the Rockford Art Museum next door - both are in the Riverfront Museum Park

map_keywords: Millennium Park Cloud Gate Bean Michigan Avenue Chicago
Art Institute of Chicago Adams Street entrance Grant Park
Chicago Architecture Center River Cruise Wacker Drive
Kingston Mines Blues Club Halsted Lincoln Park Chicago
Chicago Cultural Center Tiffany dome Randolph Michigan Avenue
Museum of Science and Industry 57th Street Hyde Park Chicago
Wicker Park Milwaukee Avenue Damen Chicago neighborhood
Chicago Lakefront Trail Divvy bike Grant Park
Al's Italian Beef Taylor Street Chicago

visits.name: Millennium Park
visits.text: You walk into a 24.5-acre park that opened in 2004 on a former rail yard and parking lot, anchored by Anish Kapoor's Cloud Gate - 110 tons of polished stainless steel reflecting the skyline. The Jay Pritzker Pavilion by Frank Gehry hosts the summer music series from the lawn. Crown Fountain's two 50-foot towers (Jaume Plensa) shoot water in summer. Lurie Garden stays in bloom spring through fall. Free admission; open daily 6am to 11pm.

practical_info.tip: arrive at sunrise for Cloud Gate photos without crowds

visits.name: Art Institute of Chicago
visits.text: 300,000 works spanning ancient Egypt to the present, including Seurat's A Sunday on La Grande Jatte, Hopper's Nighthawks, Wood's American Gothic, and van Gogh's The Bedroom - all permanent collection. The Modern Wing (2009, Renzo Piano) connects via the Nichols Bridgeway to Millennium Park. More than 1.5 million visitors annually. Paid entry; use the Monroe Street (Modern Wing) entrance to avoid the Michigan Avenue queue.

practical_info.tip: Monroe Street entrance has essentially no queue vs. the Michigan Avenue entrance

visits.name: Chicago Riverwalk
visits.text: You follow the 1.25-mile Riverwalk along the south bank of the Chicago River through the Loop, past the Wrigley Building (1924), Tribune Tower, and Marina City's corn-cob towers. Outdoor cafes, wine bars, and kayak rentals occupy old barge slips. For photos, position yourself between the Clark and LaSalle Street bridges for unobstructed canyon views of the skyscrapers rising on both sides. Free admission.

practical_info.tip: wednesday and saturday mornings in spring/fall, bridge lifts for sailboats heading to the lake are worth timing your visit around

activities.text: You board the Chicago Architecture Foundation's 90-minute docent-led river cruise from the lower Riverwalk - the cruise covers more than 50 buildings from the water, the only angle from which Chicago's layered architectural history makes complete sense. Guides cover everything from Louis Sullivan's proto-skyscrapers to Helmut Jahn's postmodern towers. Runs year-round with heated boats in winter. Weekends sell out - book in advance. Paid entry.

practical_info.tip: book 3-4 days ahead for weekend departures; weekday morning cruises are less crowded

activities.text: Chicago's neighborhood dining scene is the real argument for spending more than one day here. Pilsen (Mexican murals and taquerias), Chinatown (dim sum), and the West Loop (James Beard-level restaurants) are all within 20 minutes of the Loop by CTA. The Chicago-style hot dog - yellow mustard, white onions, green relish, tomato, pickle, sport peppers, celery salt, on a poppy seed bun - is available at Vienna Beef stands throughout the city. Deep-dish pizza at Pequod's on North Clybourn has a devoted following for its caramelized-crust preparation.

practical_info.tip: Pequod's Pizza is worth the 20-minute ride from downtown; reserve by phone for weekend visits

seo.h1_title: Iowa Backroads: From the Missouri Bluffs to the Mississippi Cliffs
seo.intro_paragraph: This eight-day linear road trip crosses Iowa from the Loess Hills above the Missouri River to the limestone bluffs of the Mississippi, passing through seven German Pietist villages founded in 1855, the birthplace of John Wayne, and a National Monument where 206 prehistoric mounds shaped like bears and birds still stand above the river. The route ends in Galena, Illinois - where Ulysses Grant worked in a leather shop before the Civil War - and finishes in Chicago. Drive it east on I-80 and you'd miss almost all of it.
seo.seo_hook: Eight days through Iowa's German communes, prehistoric mound trails, Mississippi bluffs, and Grant's hometown, ending in Chicago.
featured_list: Amana Colonies: Seven German communal villages founded in 1855, operating a working woolen mill since 1859
Effigy Mounds: 206 prehistoric earthen mounds along the Mississippi, 31 shaped like bears and birds built between 500 BCE and 1300 CE
Dubuque: Iowa's oldest city, home to the world's shortest steepest funicular and the only shot tower west of the Mississippi
Galena: 85 percent of the town is a National Historic District - Ulysses Grant lived here before and after commanding the Union Army

places_taglines.US::council-bluffs: Where Lewis and Clark held council in 1804 and Lincoln chose the transcontinental railroad's eastern terminus.
places_taglines.US::winterset: Birthplace of John Wayne and county seat of Madison County, home to six covered bridges on the National Register.
places_taglines.US::des-moines: Iowa's capital anchored by the country's only five-domed capitol, its gold leaf dome visible from miles around.
places_taglines.US::amana: Seven German Pietist villages that lived communally from 1855 to 1932, still running Iowa's only working textile mill.
places_taglines.US::cedar-rapids: Czech and Slovak immigrant city with the world's largest Grant Wood collection and North America's only Orloj.
places_taglines.US::dubuque: Iowa's oldest city stacked on Mississippi River bluffs, with a funicular built in 1882 and the only shot tower west of the Mississippi.
places_taglines.US::galena: Ulysses Grant's hometown, where 85 percent of the historic district looks as it did during the Civil War era.
places_taglines.US::rockford: Northern Illinois industrial city with an unexpected world-class Japanese garden on Spring Creek.
places_taglines.US::chicago: Where the Midwest's railroad lines, river commerce, and architectural ambition converged into the city that invented the skyscraper.

itineraries.subtitle: German communes, ancient effigy mounds, and the great river that built the Midwest

practical_context.climate_note: Iowa summers are humid and hot; spring and fall offer the best driving conditions. The Loess Hills HawkWatch runs September through December.
